questions.subject: Mathematics
questions.questionText: Urn A contains 2 white and 2 black balls while urn B contains 3 white and 2 black balls. One ball is transferred from urn A to urn B and then a ball is drawn out of urn B. What is the probability that the ball is white?
questions.solution:
Let’s label the events:

• W₁ = “white ball transferred from A”

• B₁ = “black ball transferred from A”

• W₂ = “white ball drawn from B”

We want

$$P(W₂) = P(W₂\mid W₁)\,P(W₁)\;+\;P(W₂\mid B₁)\,P(B₁).$$

From Urn A:

• P(W₁)=2/4=1/2

• P(B₁)=2/4=1/2

After transferring:

• If W₁ occurred, Urn B has 4 white, 2 black ⇒ P(W₂∣W₁)=4/6=2/3

• If B₁ occurred, Urn B has 3 white, 3 black ⇒ P(W₂∣B₁)=3/6=1/2

Putting it together:

$$P(W₂)=\frac12\cdot\frac23\;+\;\frac12\cdot\frac12 =\frac{1}{3}+\frac{1}{4} =\frac{4}{12}+\frac{3}{12} =\frac{7}{12}.$$

Answer: 7/12 (Option 2).

questions.subject: Mathematics
questions.questionText:
Box $$1$$ contains three cards bearing numbers $$1,2,3;$$ box $$2$$ contains five cards bearing numbers $$1,2,3,4,5;$$ and box $$3$$ contains seven cards bearing numbers $$1,2,3,4,5,6,7.$$ A card is drawn from each of the boxes. Let $${x_i}$$ be number on the card drawn from the $${i^{th}}$$ box, $$i=1,2,3.$$

The probability that $${x_1} + {x_2} + {x_3}$$ is odd, is
questions.solution:
Given three Boxes:

Given, that a card is drow from each Box and $$x_i$$ denote the number on the card drawn from $$i^{\text {tht }}$$ box, $$i=1,2,3$$.

Sample space $$={ }^3 \mathrm{C}_1 .{ }^5 \mathrm{C}_1 .{ }^7 \mathrm{C}_1=3 \cdot 5 \cdot 7=105$$.

Let A be the event when $$x_1+x_2+x_3$$ is odd.

Case I: When all $$x_1, x_2, x_3$$ are odd

No. of ways $$={ }^2 \mathrm{C}_1 .{ }^3 \mathrm{C}_1 .{ }^4 \mathrm{C}_1=2.3 \cdot 4=24$$

Case II: When $$x_1$$ is odd and $$x_2, x_3$$ are even

No. of ways $$={ }^2 \mathrm{C}_1 .{ }^2 \mathrm{C}_1 .{ }^3 \mathrm{C}_1=12$$

Case III: When $$x_2$$ is odd and $$x_1, x_3$$ are even

No. of ways $$={ }^1 \mathrm{C}_1 .{ }^3 \mathrm{C}_1 .{ }^3 \mathrm{C}_1=9$$

Case IV: When $$x_3$$ is odd and $$x_1, x_2$$ are even

No. of ways $$={ }^1 \mathrm{C}_1 .{ }^2 \mathrm{C}_1 . \mathrm{C}_1 \mathrm{C}_1=8$$

No. of favorable cases for A is

$$\begin{aligned} 24+12+9+8 & =53 \\ \text { Required Probability } & =\mathrm{P}(\mathrm{A})=\frac{53}{105} \end{aligned}$$

Hint:

Recall that sum of three numbers is odd only when

(i) All three numbers are odd.

(ii) Two numbers are even and one is odd.

questions.subject: Mathematics
questions.questionText: Let E, F and G be three events having probabilities $$P(E) = {1 \over 8}$$, $$P(F) = {1 \over 6}$$ and $$P(G) = {1 \over 4}$$, and let P (E $$\cap$$ F $$\cap$$ G) = $${1 \over {10}}$$. For any event H, if H<sup>c</sup> denotes the complement, then which of the following statements is (are) TRUE?
questions.solution:
For option (a)

$$P(E \cap F \cap {G^C}) = P(E \cap F) - P(E \cap F \cap G) \le P(E) - P(E \cap F \cap G)$$

$$ = {1 \over 8} - {1 \over {10}} = {1 \over {40}}$$

For option (b)

$$P({E^C} \cap F \cap G) = P(F \cap G) - P(E \cap F \cap G) \le P(F) - P(E \cap F \cap G)$$

$$ = {1 \over 6} - {1 \over {10}} = {1 \over {15}}$$

For option (c)

$$P(E \cup F \cup G) \le P(E) + P(F) + P(G)$$

$$ = {1 \over 8} + {1 \over 6} + {1 \over 4} = {{3 + 4 + 6} \over {24}} = {{13} \over {24}}$$

For option (d)

$$P({E^C} \cap {F^C} \cap {G^C}) = 1 - P(E \cap F \cap G) \ge 1 - {{13} \over {24}}$$

$$P({E^C} \cap {F^C} \cap {G^C}) \ge {{11} \over {24}} > {5 \over {12}}$$

questions.subject: Mathematics
questions.questionText:
Let X be a random variable with distribution.

x $$ - $$2 $$ - $$1 3 4 6
P(X = x) $${1 \over 5}$$ a $${1 \over 3}$$ $${1 \over 5}$$ b


If the mean of X is 2.3 and variance of X is $$\sigma$$2, then 100 $$\sigma$$2 is equal to :
questions.solution:
x $$ - $$2 $$ - $$1 3 4 6
P(X = x) $${1 \over 5}$$ a $${1 \over 3}$$ $${1 \over 5}$$ b


$$\overline X $$ = 2.3

$$-$$a + 6b = $${9 \over {10}}$$ ..... (1)

$$\sum {{P_i} = {1 \over 5} + a + {1 \over 3} + {1 \over 5} + b = 1} $$

$$a + b = {4 \over {15}}$$ .... (2)

From equation (1) and (2)

$$a = {1 \over {10}},b = {1 \over 6}$$

$${\sigma ^2} = \sum {{p_i}x_i^2 - {{(\overline X )}^2}} $$

$${1 \over 5}(4) + a(1) + {1 \over 3}(9) + {1 \over 5}(16) + b(36) - {(2.3)^2}$$

$$ = {4 \over 5} + a + 3 + {{16} \over 5} + 36b - {(2.3)^2}$$

$$ = 4 + a + 3 + 36b - {(2.3)^2}$$

$$ = 7 + a + 36b - {(2.3)^2}$$

$$ = 7 + {1 \over {10}} + 6 - {(2.3)^2}$$

$$ = 13 + {1 \over {10}} - {\left( {{{23} \over {10}}} \right)^2}$$

$$ = {{131} \over {10}} - {\left( {{{23} \over {10}}} \right)^2}$$

$$ = {{1310 - {{(23)}^2}} \over {100}}$$

$$ = {{1310 - 529} \over {100}}$$

$${\sigma ^2} = {{781} \over {100}}$$

$$100{\sigma ^2} = 781$$

questions.subject: Mathematics
questions.questionText:
Under which of the following conditions may binomial distribution be used?

I. The number of trials is infinite and not fixed.

II. The trials are independent.

III. Each trial has two possible outcomes.

Select the correct answer using the code given below.
questions.solution:
For a binomial distribution, the required conditions are:

  1. The number of trials must be fixed.

  2. The trials must be independent.

  3. Each trial must have exactly two possible outcomes.

  4. The probability of success must remain the same in every trial.

Now check the statements:

  • I. The number of trials is infinite and not fixed.

    This is false, because in binomial distribution the number of trials must be fixed.

  • II. The trials are independent.

    This is true.

  • III. Each trial has two possible outcomes.

    This is true.

So, the correct statements are II and III.

Hence, the correct answer is:

$$\boxed{\text{Option 4: II and III}}$$

questions.subject: Mathematics
questions.questionText: The probability that a randomly chosen one-one function from the set {a, b, c, d} to the set {1, 2, 3, 4, 5} satisfies f(a) + 2f(b) $$-$$ f(c) = f(d) is :
questions.solution:
Number of one-one function from $\{a, b, c, d\}$ to set $\{1,2,3,4,5\}$ is ${ }^{5} P_{4}=120 n(s)$.

The required possible set of value (f(a), $f(b), f(c), f(d))$ such that $f(a)+2 f(b)-f(c)=f(d)$ are $(5,3,2,1),(5,1,2,3),(4,1,3,5),(3,1,4,5)$, $(5,4,3,2)$ and $(3,4,5,2)$

$\therefore n(E)=6$

$\therefore $ Required probability $=\frac{n(E)}{n(S)}=\frac{6}{120}=\frac{1}{20}$

questions.subject: Mathematics
questions.questionText: A group of 40 students appeared in an examination of 3 subjects - Mathematics, Physics and Chemistry. It was found that all students passed in atleast one of the subjects, 20 students passed in Mathematics, 25 students passed in Physics, 16 students passed in Chemistry, atmost 11 students passed in both Mathematics and Physics, atmost 15 students passed in both Physics and Chemistry, atmost 15 students passed in both Mathematics and Chemistry. The maximum number of students passed in all the three subjects is _________.
questions.solution:
$\begin{aligned} & n(M)=20 \\\\ & n(P)=25 \\\\ & n(C)=16 \\\\ & n(M \cap P)=11 \\\\ & n(P \cap C)=15 \\\\ & n(M \cap C)=15\end{aligned}$

$$ \begin{aligned} & n(C \cup P \cup M) \leq n(U)=40 . \\\\ & n(C)+n(P)+n(M)-n(C \cap M)-n(P \cap M)-n(C \cap \\\\ & P)+n(C \cap P \cap M) \leq 40 \\\\ & 20+25+16-11-15-15+x \leq 40 \\\\ & x \leq 20 \end{aligned} $$

But $11-x \geq 0$ and $15-x \geq 0$

$$ \Rightarrow x \geq 11 $$

questions.subject: Mathematics
questions.questionText: Let the probability of getting head for a biased coin be $$\frac{1}{4}$$. It is tossed repeatedly until a head appears. Let $$\mathrm{N}$$ be the number of tosses required. If the probability that the equation $$64 \mathrm{x}^{2}+5 \mathrm{Nx}+1=0$$ has no real root is $$\frac{\mathrm{p}}{\mathrm{q}}$$, where $$\mathrm{p}$$ and $$\mathrm{q}$$ are coprime, then $$q-p$$ is equal to ________.
questions.solution:
We have the quadratic equation $64x^2 + 5Nx + 1 = 0$. For it to have no real roots, the discriminant ($b^2 - 4ac$) should be less than 0. Here, $a = 64$, $b = 5N$, and $c = 1$.

This gives us :

$(5N)^2 - 4\times64\times1 < 0$

$\Rightarrow 25N^2 < 256$

$\Rightarrow N^2 < \frac{256}{25}$

$\Rightarrow N < \sqrt{\frac{256}{25}} = \frac{16}{5}$

Since $N$ must be an integer (as it represents the number of tosses), the possible values of $N$ are 1, 2, or 3.

The probability of getting the first head on the $n$-th toss (given the probability of getting a head is $1/4$) is given by the geometric distribution formula, $(1 - p)^{n-1}\times p$.

So, the probability for our specific values of $N$ is:

$P(N=1) = (1 - 1/4)^{1-1}\times(1/4) = 1/4$

$P(N=2) = (1 - 1/4)^{2-1}\times(1/4) = 3/4 \times 1/4 = 3/16$

$P(N=3) = (1 - 1/4)^{3-1}\times(1/4) = (3/4)^2 \times 1/4 = 9/64$

Therefore, the total probability (p/q) is :

$p/q = P(N=1) + P(N=2) + P(N=3)$

$= 1/4 + 3/16 + 9/64$

$= 16/64 + 12/64 + 9/64$

$= 37/64$

So, $p = 37$, $q = 64$ and $q-p = 64 - 37 = 27$.

Therefore, $q-p$ is equal to $27$.

questions.subject: Mathematics
questions.questionText: What is the probability that Q and U sit together?
questions.solution:
To solve this problem, let's start by determining the total number of ways to arrange the six individuals (P, Q, R, S, T, U) in a row. Since there are 6 people, the total number of permutations is:

$$ 6! = 720 $$

Now, let's consider the number of ways Q and U can sit together. We can treat Q and U as a single unit, which means we now have 5 units to arrange (the unit containing Q and U, and the other 4 individuals P, R, S, T). The number of ways to arrange these 5 units is:

$$ 5! = 120 $$

Within the Q and U unit, Q and U can switch places. There are 2 ways to arrange Q and U within their unit:

$$ 2! = 2 $$

Therefore, the number of ways Q and U can sit together is:

$$ 5! \times 2! = 120 \times 2 = 240 $$

The probability that Q and U sit together is the number of favorable outcomes divided by the total number of outcomes:

$$ \text{Probability} = \frac{5! \times 2!}{6!} = \frac{240}{720} = \frac{1}{3} $$

Therefore, the correct option is D:

$$ \frac{1}{3} $$

questions.subject: Mathematics
questions.questionText: Three fair dice are thrown. What is the probability of getting a total greater than or equal to 15 ?
questions.solution:
Concept:

The probability of an event can be calculated using the formula:

$ \text{Probability of an event} = \frac{\text{Favorable number of outcomes}}{\text{Total number of outcomes}} $

Calculation:

When a die is tossed, the possible outcomes are {1, 2, 3, 4, 5, 6}. If three fair dice are thrown, the possibilities for the sum to be greater than or equal to 15 are as follows:

Possible sums: 15, 16, 17, 18.

The table below shows the different combinations of dice outcomes that achieve these sums:

Dice 1 Dice 2 Dice 3 Sum
3 6 6 15
4 5 6 15
4 6 5 15
5 4 6 15
5 5 5 15
5 6 4 15
6 3 6 15
6 4 5 15
6 5 4 15
6 6 3 15
4 6 6 16
5 5 6 16
5 6 5 16
6 4 6 16
6 5 5 16
6 6 4 16
5 6 6 17
6 5 6 17
6 6 5 17
6 6 6 18

The number of favorable outcomes is 20. The total number of possible outcomes when throwing three dice is $6 \times 6 \times 6 = 216$.

Therefore, the probability of getting a total sum greater than or equal to 15 is:

$ \frac{20}{216} = \frac{5}{54} $

Thus, the correct option is (D).

questions.subject: Mathematics
questions.questionText: What is the probability that P and Q take the two end positions?
questions.solution:
To determine the probability that boys P and Q take the two end positions in a row, we need to consider the total number of ways to arrange the six individuals and the number of favorable ways where P and Q are at the ends.

First, let's calculate the total number of ways to arrange the six people:

The number of permutations for 6 people is given by:

$$6! = 720$$

Now, let's calculate the number of favorable arrangements where P and Q are at the ends. There are two positions they can take: P can be at the left end and Q at the right end, or Q can be at the left end and P at the right end.

This gives us:

$$2! = 2$$

For each arrangement of P and Q at the ends, the remaining 4 people can be permuted in:

$$4! = 24$$

So, the number of favorable arrangements is:

$$2 \times 4! = 2 \times 24 = 48$$

The probability of P and Q taking the two end positions is the number of favorable arrangements divided by the total number of arrangements:

$$\frac{Number \; of \; favorable \; arrangements}{Total \; number \; of \; arrangements} = \frac{48}{720} = \frac{1}{15}$$

Therefore, the correct answer is:

Option A

$$\frac{1}{15}$$

questions.subject: Mathematics
questions.questionText: Three dice are rolled. If the probability of getting different numbers on the three dice is $$\frac{p}{q}$$, where $$p$$ and $$q$$ are co-prime, then $$q-p$$ is equal to :
questions.solution:
Total number of outcomes $=6 \times 6 \times 6=216$

Number of outcomes getting different numbers on the three dice are ${ }^6 P_3=\frac{6 !}{3 !}=120$

$\therefore$ Required probability $=\frac{120}{216}=\frac{5}{9}$

$\therefore p=5$ and $q=9$

$\therefore q-p=9-5=4$

questions.subject: Mathematics
questions.questionText: Let a computer program generate only the digits 0 and 1 to form a string of binary numbers with probability of occurrence of 0 at even places be $${1 \over 2}$$ and probability of occurrence of 0 at the odd place be $${1 \over 3}$$. Then the probability that '10' is followed by '01' is equal to :
questions.solution:
P(0 at even place) = $${1 \over 2}$$

P(0 at odd place) = $${1 \over 3}$$

P(1 at even place) = $${1 \over 2}$$

P(1 at odd place) = $${2 \over 3}$$

P(10 is followed by 01)

= $$\left( {{2 \over 3} \times {1 \over 2} \times {1 \over 3} \times {1 \over 2}} \right) + \left( {{1 \over 2} \times {1 \over 3} \times {1 \over 2} \times {2 \over 3}} \right)$$

= $${1 \over {18}} + {1 \over {18}}$$

= $${1 \over 9}$$

questions.subject: Mathematics
questions.questionText: Two number $\mathrm{k}_1$ and $\mathrm{k}_2$ are randomly chosen from the set of natural numbers. Then, the probability that the value of $\mathrm{i}^{\mathrm{k}_1}+\mathrm{i}^{\mathrm{k}_2},(\mathrm{i}=\sqrt{-1})$ is non-zero, equals
questions.solution:
$i^{k_1}+i^{k_2} \neq 0 \quad i^{k_1} \rightarrow 4$ option for $\mathrm{i},-1,-\mathrm{i}, 1$

Total cases $\Rightarrow 4 \times 4=16$

Unfovourble cases $\Rightarrow \mathrm{i}^{\mathrm{k}_1}+\mathrm{i}^{\mathrm{k}_2}=0$

$$\left\{\begin{array}{c} 1,-1 \\ -1,1 \\ i,-i \\ -i, i \end{array}\right\}$$

4 Cases $\Rightarrow$ Probability $=\frac{16-4}{16}=\frac{3}{4}$

questions.subject: Mathematics
questions.questionText: Let X be a random variable following binomial distribution whose mean and variance are 200 and 160 respectively. What is the value of the number of trials (n)?
questions.solution:
For a binomial distribution with parameters $n$ (number of trials) and $p$ (probability of success):

The mean is $np$.

The variance is $np(1-p)$.

We know:

Mean $= np = 200$

Variance $= np(1-p) = 160$

Let's solve for $n$ step by step.

Express $p$ using the mean:

$$ np = 200 \implies p = \frac{200}{n} $$

Substitute $p$ into the variance formula:

$$ np(1-p) = 160 $$

$$ n \cdot \frac{200}{n} \left(1 - \frac{200}{n}\right) = 160 $$

$$ 200 \left(1 - \frac{200}{n}\right) = 160 $$

Solve for $n$:

$$ 200 - \frac{200 \times 200}{n} = 160 $$

$$ 200 - 160 = \frac{40000}{n} $$

$$ 40 = \frac{40000}{n} $$

$$ n = \frac{40000}{40} $$

$$ n = 1000 $$

Answer:

$\boxed{1000}$

So, the correct option is Option B (1000).
